title: Six Day War

infobox.value: [Egypt](/wiki/United_Arab_Republic "United Arab Republic")  [Syria](/wiki/Ba%27athist_Syria "Ba'athist Syria")  [Jordan](/wiki/Jordan "Jordan")  [Iraq](/wiki/First_Republic_of_Iraq "First Republic of Iraq")[[1]](#cite_note-1) **Minor involvement:**  [Lebanon](/wiki/Lebanon "Lebanon")[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOren2002237-2)

infobox.key: [Levi Eshkol](/wiki/Levi_Eshkol "Levi Eshkol")  [Moshe Dayan](/wiki/Moshe_Dayan "Moshe Dayan")  [Yitzhak Rabin](/wiki/Yitzhak_Rabin "Yitzhak Rabin")  [David Elazar](/wiki/David_Elazar "David Elazar")  [Uzi Narkiss](/wiki/Uzi_Narkiss "Uzi Narkiss")  [Israel Tal](/wiki/Israel_Tal "Israel Tal")
infobox.value: [Gamal Abdel Nasser](/wiki/Gamal_Abdel_Nasser "Gamal Abdel Nasser")  [Abdel Hakim Amer](/wiki/Abdel_Hakim_Amer "Abdel Hakim Amer")  [Abdul Munim Riad](/wiki/Abdul_Munim_Riad "Abdul Munim Riad")  [King Hussein](/wiki/Hussein_of_Jordan "Hussein of Jordan")  [Abdul Rahman Arif](/wiki/Abdul_Rahman_Arif "Abdul Rahman Arif")

(5–10 June 1967), was fought between [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel") and a coalition of [Arab states](/wiki/Arab_world "Arab world"), primarily [Egypt](/wiki/United_Arab_Republic "United Arab Republic"), [Syria](/wiki/Ba%27athist_Syria "Ba'athist Syria"), and [Jordan](/wiki/Jordan "Jordan"), in the context of the [Arab–Israeli conflict](/wiki/Arab%E2%80%93Israeli_conflict "Arab–Israeli conflict"). In the war, Israel captured and [occupied](/wiki/Israeli-occupied_territories "Israeli-occupied territories") the [West Bank](/wiki/Israeli_occupation_of_the_West_Bank "Israeli occupation of the West Bank") (including [East Jerusalem](/wiki/East_Jerusalem "East Jerusalem")) from [Jordan](/wiki/Jordan "Jordan"), the [Gaza Strip](/wiki/Israeli_occupation_of_the_Gaza_Strip "Israeli occupation of the Gaza Strip") and the [Sinai Peninsula](/wiki/Israeli_occupation_of_the_Sinai_Peninsula "Israeli occupation of the Sinai Peninsula") from [Egypt](/wiki/United_Arab_Republic "United Arab Republic"), and the [Golan Heights](/wiki/Golan_Heights "Golan Heights") from [Syria](/wiki/Ba%27athist_Syria "Ba'athist Syria").

The **Six-Day War**,[[a]](#cite_note-41) or the **1967 Arab–Israeli war** (5–10 June 1967), was fought between [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel") and a coalition of [Arab states](/wiki/Arab_world "Arab world"), primarily [Egypt](/wiki/United_Arab_Republic "United Arab Republic"), [Syria](/wiki/Ba%27athist_Syria "Ba'athist Syria"), and [Jordan](/wiki/Jordan "Jordan"), in the context of the [Arab–Israeli conflict](/wiki/Arab%E2%80%93Israeli_conflict "Arab–Israeli conflict"). In the war, Israel captured and [occupied](/wiki/Israeli-occupied_territories "Israeli-occupied territories") the [West Bank](/wiki/Israeli_occupation_of_the_West_Bank "Israeli occupation of the West Bank") (including [East Jerusalem](/wiki/East_Jerusalem "East Jerusalem")) from [Jordan](/wiki/Jordan "Jordan"), the [Gaza Strip](/wiki/Israeli_occupation_of_the_Gaza_Strip "Israeli occupation of the Gaza Strip") and the [Sinai Peninsula](/wiki/Israeli_occupation_of_the_Sinai_Peninsula "Israeli occupation of the Sinai Peninsula") from [Egypt](/wiki/United_Arab_Republic "United Arab Republic"), and the [Golan Heights](/wiki/Golan_Heights "Golan Heights") from [Syria](/wiki/Ba%27athist_Syria "Ba'athist Syria").

Military hostilities broke out amid poor relations between Israel and its Arab neighbors, who had been observing the [1949 Armistice Agreements](/wiki/1949_Armistice_Agreements "1949 Armistice Agreements") signed at the end of the [First Arab–Israeli War](/wiki/1948_Arab%E2%80%93Israeli_War "1948 Arab–Israeli War"). In 1956, regional tensions over the [Straits of Tiran](/wiki/Straits_of_Tiran "Straits of Tiran") (giving access to [Eilat](/wiki/Eilat "Eilat"), a port on Israel's southeastern tip) escalated in what became known as the [Suez Crisis](/wiki/Suez_Crisis "Suez Crisis"), when Israel invaded Egypt over the [Egyptian closure of maritime passageways to Israeli shipping](/wiki/Israeli_passage_through_the_Suez_Canal_and_Straits_of_Tiran "Israeli passage through the Suez Canal and Straits of Tiran"), ultimately resulting in the reopening of the Straits of Tiran to Israel and the deployment of the [United Nations Emergency Force](/wiki/United_Nations_Emergency_Force "United Nations Emergency Force") (UNEF) along the [Egypt–Israel border](/wiki/Borders_of_Israel#Border_with_Egypt "Borders of Israel").[[41]](#cite_note-Rikhye2013-42)

In the months before June 1967, [tensions again became dangerously heightened](/wiki/Origins_of_the_Six-Day_War "Origins of the Six-Day War"): Israel reiterated its post-1956 position that another Egyptian closure of the Straits of Tiran to Israeli shipping would be a definite *[casus belli](/wiki/Casus_belli "Casus belli")*. In May, Egyptian president [Gamal Abdel Nasser](/wiki/Gamal_Abdel_Nasser "Gamal Abdel Nasser") announced that the Straits of Tiran would again be closed to Israeli vessels. He mobilized the Egyptian military into defensive lines along the border with Israel[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQuigley2013[httpsbooksgooglecombooksid0zEi3qGWLFICpgPA32_32]-43) and ordered the immediate withdrawal of all UNEF personnel.[[43]](#cite_note-44)[[35]](#cite_note-Question_of_Palestine-35)

The war began on 5 June 1967 with [Operation Focus](/wiki/Operation_Focus "Operation Focus"), an Israeli [surprise attack](/wiki/Military_deception "Military deception") consisting of a series of airstrikes against Egyptian airfields and other facilities as the UNEF was leaving the zone.[[35]](#cite_note-Question_of_Palestine-35) Israel caught Egyptian forces by surprise and destroyed nearly all of Egypt's military aerial assets, giving Israel [air supremacy](/wiki/Air_supremacy "Air supremacy"). Simultaneously, the Israeli military launched a ground offensive into Egypt's [Sinai Peninsula](/wiki/Sinai_Peninsula "Sinai Peninsula") and the [Egyptian-occupied Gaza Strip](/wiki/Occupation_of_the_Gaza_Strip_by_the_United_Arab_Republic "Occupation of the Gaza Strip by the United Arab Republic"). After some initial resistance, Nasser ordered an evacuation of the Sinai Peninsula; by the sixth day of the conflict, [Israel had occupied the entire Sinai Peninsula](/wiki/Israeli_occupation_of_the_Sinai_Peninsula "Israeli occupation of the Sinai Peninsula").[[44]](#cite_note-BBC_Panorama-45) Jordan, which had entered into a defense pact with Egypt a week before the war began, did not take on an all-out offensive role against Israel, but launched attacks against Israeli forces to slow their advance.[[45]](#cite_note-46) On the fifth day, Syria joined the war by shelling Israeli positions in the north.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDunstan2013[httpsbooksgooglecombooksid8Vy1CwAAQBAJpgPA65_65]-47)

Egypt and Jordan agreed to a ceasefire on 8 June, and Syria on 9 June, and it was signed with Israel on 11 June. The Six-Day War resulted in more than 15,000 Arab fatalities; Israel suffered fewer than 1,000. Alongside the combatant casualties were the deaths of 20 Israeli civilians killed in Arab forces air strikes on Jerusalem, 15 [UN peacekeepers](/wiki/UN_peacekeepers "UN peacekeepers") killed by Israeli strikes in the Sinai at the outset of the war, and 34 US personnel killed in the [USS *Liberty* incident](/wiki/USS_Liberty_incident "USS Liberty incident"), in which Israeli air and naval forces assaulted a [United States Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy") [technical research ship](/wiki/Technical_research_ship "Technical research ship").

When hostilities ceased, Israel had occupied the [Golan Heights](/wiki/Golan_Heights "Golan Heights") from Syria, the [West Bank](/wiki/West_Bank "West Bank") including [East Jerusalem](/wiki/East_Jerusalem "East Jerusalem") from Jordan, and the Sinai Peninsula and the [Gaza Strip](/wiki/Gaza_Strip "Gaza Strip") from Egypt. The displacement of civilian populations as a result of the Six-Day War had long-term consequences, as around 280,000 to 325,000 [Palestinians](/wiki/Palestinians "Palestinians") and 100,000 Syrians [fled or were expelled](/wiki/1967_Palestinian_exodus "1967 Palestinian exodus") from the West Bank[[47]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBowker200381-48) and the Golan Heights, respectively.[[48]](#cite_note-McDowall_p64-49) Nasser resigned in shame after Israel's victory, but was reinstated after a series of protests across Egypt. In the aftermath of the conflict, [Egypt closed the Suez Canal from 1967 to 1975](/wiki/Closure_of_the_Suez_Canal_(1967%E2%80%931975) "Closure of the Suez Canal (1967–1975)").[[49]](#cite_note-50)

## Background

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Nasser_and_Egyptian_pilots_pre-1967.gif/250px-Nasser_and_Egyptian_pilots_pre-1967.gif)](/wiki/File:Nasser_and_Egyptian_pilots_pre-1967.gif)

On 22 May 1967, President Nasser addressed his pilots at [Bir Gifgafa Airfield](/wiki/Bir_Gifgafa_Airfield "Bir Gifgafa Airfield") in Sinai: "The Jews are threatening war—we say to them [ahlan wa-sahlan](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%D8%A3%D9%87%D9%84%D8%A7_%D9%88%D8%B3%D9%87%D9%84%D8%A7 "wikt:أهلا وسهلا") (welcome)!"[[50]](#cite_note-Gluska2007p152-51)

After the 1956 [Suez Crisis](/wiki/Suez_Crisis "Suez Crisis"), Egypt agreed to the stationing of a [United Nations Emergency Force](/wiki/United_Nations_Emergency_Force "United Nations Emergency Force") (UNEF) in the Sinai to ensure all parties would comply with the [1949 Armistice Agreements](/wiki/1949_Armistice_Agreements "1949 Armistice Agreements").[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERauschningWiesbrockLailach199730-52)[[52]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESachar2007504,_507–508-53)[[53]](#cite_note-54) In the following years, there were numerous minor border clashes between Israel and its Arab neighbors, particularly Syria. In early November 1966, Syrian president [Nureddin al-Atassi](/wiki/Nureddin_al-Atassi "Nureddin al-Atassi") signed a [mutual defense agreement](/wiki/Collective_security "Collective security") with Egypt.[[54]](#cite_note-55) Soon thereafter, in response to [Palestine Liberation Organisation](/wiki/Palestine_Liberation_Organisation "Palestine Liberation Organisation") (PLO) guerilla activity,[[55]](#cite_note-56)[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChurchillChurchill196721-57) including a mine attack that left three dead,[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPollack2004290-58) the Israeli Defense Force (IDF) [attacked the village](/wiki/Samu_Incident "Samu Incident") of [as-Samu](/wiki/As-Samu "As-Samu") in the Jordanian-ruled West Bank.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESegev2007149–152-59) Jordanian units that engaged the Israelis were quickly beaten back.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHart1989226-60) King [Hussein of Jordan](/wiki/Hussein_of_Jordan "Hussein of Jordan") criticized Egyptian President [Gamal Abdel Nasser](/wiki/Gamal_Abdel_Nasser "Gamal Abdel Nasser") for failing to come to Jordan's aid and "hiding behind UNEF skirts".[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOren2002312-61)[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBurrowesMuzzio1972224–225-62)[[62]](#cite_note-shemesh2007p118-63)[[63]](#cite_note-64)

In May 1967, Nasser received false reports from the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") that Israel was massing on the Syrian border.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHerzog1982148-65) Nasser began massing his troops in two defensive lines[[42]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQuigley2013[httpsbooksgooglecombooksid0zEi3qGWLFICpgPA32_32]-43) in the Sinai Peninsula on Israel's border (16 May), expelled the UNEF force from Gaza and Sinai (19 May), and took over UNEF positions at [Sharm el-Sheikh](/wiki/Sharm_el-Sheikh "Sharm el-Sheikh"), overlooking the [Straits of Tiran](/wiki/Straits_of_Tiran "Straits of Tiran").[[65]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShlaim2007238-66)[[66]](#cite_note-67) Israel repeated declarations it had made in 1957 that any closure of the Straits would be considered an act of war or justification for war,[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECohen198812-68)[[68]](#cite_note-MeirStraitsSpeech-69) but on 22-23 May, Nasser ordered the Straits to be closed to Israeli shipping.[[69]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorris1999306-70)[[70]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGat2003[httpsbooksgooglecombooksidntLdA8QIgXICpgPA202_202]-71)[[71]](#cite_note-Colonomos2013p25-72) After the war, U.S. President [Lyndon B. Johnson](/wiki/Lyndon_B._Johnson "Lyndon B. Johnson") said:[[72]](#cite_note-73)

> If a single act of folly was more responsible for this explosion than any other, it was the arbitrary and dangerous announced decision that the Straits of Tiran would be closed. The right of innocent, maritime passage must be preserved for all nations.

On 30 May, Jordan and Egypt signed a defense pact. On 31 May, having earlier declined Hussein's invitation to send troops to Jordan, Iraqi president [Abdul Rahman Arif](/wiki/Abdul_Rahman_Arif "Abdul Rahman Arif") agreed to deploy troops and armored units in Jordan to confront Israel.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEChurchillChurchill196752_&_77-74) They were later reinforced by an Egyptian contingent. On 1 June, Israel formed a [National Unity Government](/wiki/National_Unity_Government "National Unity Government") by widening its cabinet, and on 4 June the decision was made to go to war. The next morning, Israel launched [Operation Focus](/wiki/Operation_Focus "Operation Focus"), the surprise air strike that launched the war.

### Military preparation

Before the war, Israeli pilots and ground crews had trained extensively in rapid refitting of aircraft returning from [sorties](/wiki/Sortie "Sortie"), enabling a single aircraft to sortie up to four times a day, as opposed to the norm in Arab air forces of one or two. This enabled the [Israeli Air Force](/wiki/Israeli_Air_Force "Israeli Air Force") (IAF) to send several attack waves against Egyptian airfields on the war's first day, overwhelming the Egyptian Air Force and allowing the IAF to knock out other Arab air forces the same day. This has contributed to the Arab belief that the IAF was helped by foreign air forces (see [Controversies relating to the Six-Day War](/wiki/Controversies_relating_to_the_Six-Day_War#Combat_support "Controversies relating to the Six-Day War")). Pilots were extensively schooled about their targets, memorized layouts in detail, and rehearsed the operation multiple times on dummy runways in total secrecy.

The Egyptians had constructed fortified defenses in the Sinai. These designs were based on the assumption that an attack would come along the few roads leading through the desert rather than through the difficult desert terrain. The Israelis chose not to risk attacking the Egyptian defenses head-on, instead surprising them from an unexpected direction.

[James Reston](/wiki/James_Reston "James Reston") wrote in *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* on 23 May 1967: "In discipline, training, morale, equipment and general competence his [Nasser's] army and the other Arab forces, without the direct assistance of the Soviet Union, are no match for the Israelis. ... Even with 50,000 troops and the best of his generals and air force in Yemen, he has not been able to work his way in that small and primitive country, and even his effort to help the Congo rebels was a flop."[[74]](#cite_note-75)

On the eve of the war, Israel believed it could win a war in 3–4 days. The United States estimated Israel would need 7–10 days, with British estimates supporting the U.S. view.[[75]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEQuigley2013[httpsbooksgooglecombooksid0zEi3qGWLFICpgPA60_60]-76)[[76]](#cite_note-77)

## Armies and weapons

### Armies

The Israeli army had a total strength, including reservists, of 264,000, though this number could not be sustained during a long conflict, as the reservists were vital to civilian life.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStone2004217-6)

Against Jordan's forces on the [West Bank](/wiki/West_Bank "West Bank"), Israel deployed about 40,000 troops and 200 tanks (eight brigades).[[77]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPollack2004294-78) Israeli Central Command forces consisted of five brigades. The first two were permanently stationed near [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem") and were the [Jerusalem Brigade](/wiki/16th_Infantry_Brigade_(Israel) "16th Infantry Brigade (Israel)") and the mechanized [Harel Brigade](/wiki/Harel_Brigade "Harel Brigade"). [Mordechai Gur](/wiki/Mordechai_Gur "Mordechai Gur")'s [55th Paratroopers Brigade](/wiki/55th_Paratroopers_Brigade "55th Paratroopers Brigade") was summoned from the Sinai front. The 10th Armored Brigade was stationed north of the West Bank. The Israeli Northern Command comprised a division of three brigades led by Major General [Elad Peled](/wiki/Elad_Peled "Elad Peled") which was stationed in the [Jezreel Valley](/wiki/Jezreel_Valley "Jezreel Valley") to the north of the West Bank.

On the eve of the war, Egypt massed approximately 100,000 of its 160,000 troops in the Sinai, including all seven of its divisions (four infantry, two armored and one mechanized), four independent infantry brigades and four independent armored brigades. Over a third of these soldiers were veterans of Egypt's continuing intervention into the [North Yemen Civil War](/wiki/North_Yemen_Civil_War "North Yemen Civil War") and another third were reservists. These forces had 950 tanks, 1,100 APCs, and more than 1,000 artillery pieces.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPollack200459-10)

Syria's army had a total strength of 75,000 and was deployed along the border with Israel.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEhteshamiHinnebusch199776-13) Professor [David W. Lesch](/wiki/David_W._Lesch "David W. Lesch") wrote that "One would be hard-pressed to find a military less prepared for war with a clearly superior foe" since Syria's army had been decimated in the months and years prior through coups and attempted coups that had resulted in a series of purges, fracturings and uprisings within the armed forces.[[78]](#cite_note-79)

The [Jordanian Armed Forces](/wiki/Jordanian_Armed_Forces "Jordanian Armed Forces") included 11 brigades, totaling 55,000 troops.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMutawi200242-14) Nine brigades (45,000 troops, 270 tanks, 200 artillery pieces) were deployed in the [West Bank](/wiki/West_Bank "West Bank"), including the elite armored 40th, and two in the [Jordan Valley](/wiki/Jordan_Valley_(Middle_East) "Jordan Valley (Middle East)"). They possessed sizable numbers of [M113](/wiki/M113_Armored_Personnel_Carrier "M113 Armored Personnel Carrier") APCs and were equipped with some 300 modern Western tanks, 250 of which were U.S. [M48 Pattons](/wiki/M48_Patton "M48 Patton"). They also had 12 battalions of artillery, six batteries of 81 mm and 120 mm mortars,[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESegev196782,_175–191-15) a [paratrooper](/wiki/Paratrooper "Paratrooper") battalion trained in the new U.S.-built school and a new battalion of [mechanized infantry](/wiki/Mechanized_infantry "Mechanized infantry"). The [Jordanian Army](/wiki/Jordanian_Army "Jordanian Army") was a long-term-service, professional army, relatively well-equipped and well-trained. Israeli post-war briefings said that the Jordanian staff acted professionally, but was always left "half a step" behind by the Israeli moves. The small [Royal Jordanian Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Jordanian_Air_Force "Royal Jordanian Air Force") consisted of only 24 British-made [Hawker Hunter](/wiki/Hawker_Hunter "Hawker Hunter") fighters, six [transport aircraft](/wiki/Military_transport_aircraft "Military transport aircraft") and two helicopters. According to the Israelis, the Hawker Hunter was essentially on par with the French-built [Dassault Mirage III](/wiki/Dassault_Mirage_III "Dassault Mirage III") – the IAF's best plane.[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPollack2004293–94-80)

One hundred Iraqi tanks and an infantry division were readied near the Jordanian border. Two squadrons of Iraqi fighter-aircraft, Hawker Hunters and [MiG 21s](/wiki/Mikoyan-Gurevich_MiG-21 "Mikoyan-Gurevich MiG-21"), were rebased adjacent to the Jordanian border.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESegev196782,_175–191-15)

In the weeks leading up to the war, Saudi Arabia mobilized forces for deployment to the Jordanian front. A Saudi infantry battalion entered Jordan on 6 June, followed by another on 8 June. Both were based in Jordan's southernmost city, [Ma'an](/wiki/Ma%27an "Ma'an"). By 17 June, the Saudi contingent in Jordan had grown to include a single infantry brigade, a tank company, two artillery batteries, a heavy mortar company, and a maintenance and support unit. By the end of July, a second tank company and a third artillery battery had been added. These forces remained in Jordan until the end of 1977, when they were recalled for re-equipment and retraining in the Karak region near the Dead Sea.[[80]](#cite_note-81)[[81]](#cite_note-sauditw-82)[[82]](#cite_note-SAMAAI-83)

The Arab air forces were reinforced by aircraft from Libya, Algeria, Morocco, Kuwait, and Saudi Arabia to make up for the massive losses suffered on the first day of the war.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] They were also aided by volunteer pilots from the [Pakistan Air Force](/wiki/Pakistan_Air_Force "Pakistan Air Force") acting in an independent capacity. PAF pilots like [Saiful Azam](/wiki/Saiful_Azam "Saiful Azam") shot down several Israeli planes.[[83]](#cite_note-pak-84)[[84]](#cite_note-saif-85)

### Weapons

With the exception of Jordan, the Arabs relied principally on Soviet weaponry. Jordan's army was equipped with American weaponry, and its air force was composed of British aircraft.

Egypt had by far the largest and the most modern of all the Arab air forces, consisting of about 420 combat aircraft,[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOren2002176-12)[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMorris2001318-11) all of them Soviet-built and with a large number of top-of-the-line [MiG-21s](/wiki/Mikoyan-Gurevich_MiG-21 "Mikoyan-Gurevich MiG-21"). Of particular concern to the Israelis were the 30 [Tu-16 "Badger"](/wiki/Tupolev_Tu-16 "Tupolev Tu-16") [medium bombers](/wiki/Medium_bomber "Medium bomber"), capable of inflicting heavy damage on Israeli military and civilian centers.[[85]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPollack200458-86)

Israeli weapons were mainly of Western origin. Its air force was composed principally of French aircraft, while its armored units were mostly of British and American design and manufacture. Some light infantry weapons, including the ubiquitous [Uzi](/wiki/Uzi "Uzi"), were of Israeli origin.

#### Nuclear weapons

These paragraphs are an excerpt from [Israel and nuclear weapons § Weapons production, 1966–present](/wiki/Israel_and_nuclear_weapons#Weapons_production,_1966–present "Israel and nuclear weapons").[[edit](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Israel_and_nuclear_weapons&action=edit)]

Israel is believed to have begun full-scale production of nuclear weapons following the 1967 Six-Day War, although it had built its first operational nuclear weapon by December 1966.[[88]](#cite_note-Israel_and_nuclear_weapons_auto-89) A [Central Intelligence Agency](/wiki/Central_Intelligence_Agency "Central Intelligence Agency") (CIA) report from early 1967 stated that Israel had the materials to construct a bomb in six to eight weeks[[89]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECohen1998298-90) and some authors suggest that Israel had two crude bombs ready for use during the war.[[90]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFarr1999-91) According to US journalist [Seymour Hersh](/wiki/Seymour_Hersh "Seymour Hersh"), everything was ready for production at this time save an official order to do so. Israel crossed the nuclear threshold on the eve of the Six-Day War in May 1967.[[91]](#cite_note-Israel_and_nuclear_weapons_6daywarstart-92) "[Prime Minister] [Eshkol](/wiki/Levi_Eshkol "Levi Eshkol"), according to a number of Israeli sources, secretly ordered the Dimona [nuclear reactor] scientists to assemble two crude nuclear devices. He placed them under the command of Brigadier General [Yitzhak Yaakov](/w/index.php?title=Yitzhak_Yaakov&action=edit&redlink=1 "Yitzhak Yaakov (page does not exist)") [[he](https://he.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D7%99%D7%A6%D7%97%D7%A7_%D7%99%D7%A2%D7%A7%D7%91 "he:יצחק יעקב")], the chief of research and development in Israel's Defense Ministry. The crude atomic bombs were readied for deployment on trucks that could race to the Egyptian border for detonation in the event Arab forces overwhelmed Israeli defenses."[[91]](#cite_note-Israel_and_nuclear_weapons_6daywarstart-92) Spider ([Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language"): עכביש) was the name for the devices.[[92]](#cite_note-Israel_and_nuclear_weapons_Ronen_Bergman_רונן_ברגמן_2017-93) The name for the plan was *Operation Shimshon* ([Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language"): מבצע שמשון), named after the [death of Samson](/wiki/Samson#Death "Samson") in [Judges 16:30](/wiki/Judges_16:30 "Judges 16:30").[[92]](#cite_note-Israel_and_nuclear_weapons_Ronen_Bergman_רונן_ברגמן_2017-93)[[93]](#cite_note-94) It was also referred to as *Operation Samson*,[[94]](#cite_note-95)[[95]](#cite_note-96)
*codename "Samson"* ([Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language"): שם הקוד "שמשון"),[[96]](#cite_note-97)[[97]](#cite_note-98)
or ([Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language"): מבצע שמשון, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Hebrew "Romanization of Hebrew"): *Mivtza Shimshon*).[[92]](#cite_note-Israel_and_nuclear_weapons_Ronen_Bergman_רונן_ברגמן_2017-93)[[98]](#cite_note-99) General Yaakov also sometimes called the plan *[Operation Doomsday](/w/index.php?title=Operation_Doomsday_(1967)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Operation Doomsday (1967) (page does not exist)") [[he](https://he.wikipedia.org/wiki/%D7%9E%D7%91%D7%A6%D7%A2_%D7%99%D7%95%D7%9D_%D7%94%D7%93%D7%99%D7%9F "he:מבצע יום הדין")]*. ([Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language"): מבצע יום הדין, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Hebrew "Romanization of Hebrew"): *Mivtza Yum Hadin*).[[99]](#cite_note-100)[[100]](#cite_note-101)[[101]](#cite_note-102)

## Fighting fronts

### Initial attack

Main article: [Operation Focus](/wiki/Operation_Focus "Operation Focus")

See also: [Order of battle for the Six-Day War](/wiki/Order_of_battle_for_the_Six-Day_War "Order of battle for the Six-Day War")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/6dayswar1.jpg/250px-6dayswar1.jpg)](/wiki/File:6dayswar1.jpg)

Israeli troops examine destroyed Egyptian aircraft

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Hatzerim_Mirage_20100129_1.jpg/250px-Hatzerim_Mirage_20100129_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hatzerim_Mirage_20100129_1.jpg)

Dassault Mirage at the [Israeli Air Force Museum](/wiki/Israeli_Air_Force_Museum "Israeli Air Force Museum"). Operation Focus was mainly conducted using French built aircraft.

The first and most critical move of the conflict was a surprise Israeli attack on the [Egyptian Air Force](/wiki/Egyptian_Air_Force "Egyptian Air Force"). Initially, both Egypt and Israel announced that they had been attacked by the other country.[[102]](#cite_note-Quigley2005p163-103)

On 5 June at 7:45 Israeli time, with [civil defense sirens](/wiki/Civil_defense_siren "Civil defense siren") sounding all over Israel, the IAF launched Operation Focus (*Moked*). All but 12 of its nearly 200 operational jets[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOren2002172-104) launched a mass attack against Egypt's [airfields](/wiki/Aerodrome "Aerodrome").[[104]](#cite_note-105) The Egyptian defensive infrastructure was extremely poor, and no airfields were yet equipped with [hardened aircraft shelters](/wiki/Hardened_aircraft_shelter "Hardened aircraft shelter") capable of protecting Egypt's warplanes. Most of the Israeli warplanes headed out over the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea"), flying low to avoid radar detection, before turning toward Egypt. Others flew over the [Red Sea](/wiki/Red_Sea "Red Sea").[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOren2002eSection_"The_War:_Day_One,_June_5"-106)

Meanwhile, the Egyptians hindered their own defense by effectively shutting down their entire air defense system: they were worried that rebel Egyptian forces would shoot down the plane carrying Field Marshal [Abdel Hakim Amer](/wiki/Abdel_Hakim_Amer "Abdel Hakim Amer") and Lt-Gen. Sidqi Mahmoud, who were en route from al Maza to Bir Tamada in the [Sinai](/wiki/Sinai_Peninsula "Sinai Peninsula") to meet the commanders of the troops stationed there. It did not make a great deal of difference as the Israeli pilots came in below Egyptian [radar](/wiki/Radar "Radar") cover and well below the lowest point at which its [SA-2](/wiki/S-75_Dvina "S-75 Dvina") surface-to-air missile batteries could bring down an aircraft.[[106]](#cite_note-107)

Although the powerful Jordanian radar facility at [Ajloun](/wiki/Ajloun "Ajloun") detected waves of aircraft approaching Egypt and reported the code word for "war" up the Egyptian command chain, Egyptian command and communications problems prevented the warning from reaching the targeted airfields.[[105]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOren2002eSection_"The_War:_Day_One,_June_5"-106) The Israelis employed a mixed-attack strategy: bombing and [strafing](/wiki/Strafing "Strafing") runs against planes parked on the ground, and bombing to disable runways with special [tarmac-shredding penetration bombs](/wiki/Tarmac-shredding_penetration_bomb "Tarmac-shredding penetration bomb") developed jointly with France, leaving surviving aircraft unable to take off.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOren2002[httpsarchiveorgdetailssixdaysofwarjune0000orenpage171_171]-7)

The runway at the [Arish](/wiki/Arish "Arish") airfield was spared, as the Israelis expected to turn it into a military airport for their transports after the war. Surviving aircraft were taken out by later attack waves. The operation was more successful than expected, catching the Egyptians by surprise and destroying virtually all of the Egyptian Air Force on the ground, with few Israeli losses. Only four unarmed Egyptian training flights were in the air when the strike began.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOren2002[httpsarchiveorgdetailssixdaysofwarjune0000orenpage171_171]-7) A total of 338 Egyptian aircraft were destroyed and 100 pilots were killed,[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPollack2005474-108) although the number of aircraft lost by the Egyptians is disputed.[[32]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-32)

Among the Egyptian planes lost were all 30 [Tu-16](/wiki/Tu-16 "Tu-16") bombers, 27 out of 40 [Il-28](/wiki/Ilyushin_Il-28 "Ilyushin Il-28") bombers, 12 [Su-7](/wiki/Su-7 "Su-7") fighter-bombers, over 90 [MiG-21s](/wiki/MiG-21 "MiG-21"), 20 [MiG-19s](/wiki/MiG-19 "MiG-19"), 25 [MiG-17](/wiki/MiG-17 "MiG-17") fighters, and around 32 transport planes and helicopters. In addition, Egyptian radars and SAM missiles were also attacked and destroyed. The Israelis lost 19 planes, including two destroyed in air-to-air combat and 13 downed by anti-aircraft artillery.[[108]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELong198419,_Table_1-109) One Israeli plane, which was damaged and unable to break radio silence, was shot down by Israeli [Hawk missiles](/wiki/MIM-23_Hawk "MIM-23 Hawk") after it strayed over the [Negev Nuclear Research Center](/wiki/Negev_Nuclear_Research_Center "Negev Nuclear Research Center").[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOren2002178-110) Another was destroyed by an exploding Egyptian bomber.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOren2002175-111)

The attack guaranteed Israeli [air supremacy](/wiki/Air_supremacy "Air supremacy") for the rest of the war. Attacks on other Arab air forces by Israel took place later in the day as hostilities broke out on other fronts.

The large numbers of Arab aircraft claimed destroyed by Israel on that day were at first regarded as "greatly exaggerated" by the Western press, but the fact that the Egyptian Air Force, along with other Arab air forces attacked by Israel, made practically no appearance for the remaining days of the conflict proved that the numbers were most likely authentic. Throughout the war, Israeli aircraft continued strafing Arab airfield runways to prevent their return to usability. Meanwhile, Egyptian state-run radio had reported an Egyptian victory, falsely claiming that 70 Israeli planes had been downed on the first day of fighting.[[111]](#cite_note-npr-112)

### Gaza Strip and Sinai Peninsula

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/1967_Six_Day_War_-_conquest_of_Sinai_5-6_June.jpg/500px-1967_Six_Day_War_-_conquest_of_Sinai_5-6_June.jpg)](/wiki/File:1967_Six_Day_War_-_conquest_of_Sinai_5-6_June.jpg)

The capture of Sinai. 5–6 June 1967

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/PikiWiki_Israel_16401_Israel_Defense_Forces.jpg/250px-PikiWiki_Israel_16401_Israel_Defense_Forces.jpg)](/wiki/File:PikiWiki_Israel_16401_Israel_Defense_Forces.jpg)

People in a bomb shelter at [Kfar Maimon](/wiki/Kfar_Maimon "Kfar Maimon")

The Egyptian forces consisted of seven [divisions](/wiki/Division_(military) "Division (military)"): four [armored](/wiki/Division_(military)#Armored_division "Division (military)"), two [infantry](/wiki/Division_(military)#Armored_division "Division (military)"), and one [mechanized infantry](/wiki/Mechanized_infantry "Mechanized infantry"). Overall, Egypt had around 100,000 troops and 900–950 [tanks](/wiki/Tank "Tank") in the Sinai, backed by 1,100 [APCs](/wiki/Armored_personnel_carrier "Armored personnel carrier") and 1,000 [artillery](/wiki/Artillery "Artillery") pieces.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPollack200459-10) This arrangement was thought to be based on the Soviet doctrine, where mobile armor units at [strategic depth](/wiki/Strategic_depth "Strategic depth") provide a dynamic defense while infantry units engage in defensive battles.

Israeli forces concentrated on the border with Egypt included six armored [brigades](/wiki/Brigade "Brigade"), one infantry brigade, one mechanized infantry brigade, three [paratrooper](/wiki/Paratrooper "Paratrooper") brigades, giving a total of around 70,000 men and 700 tanks, who were organized in three armored divisions. They had massed on the border the night before the war, camouflaging themselves and observing radio silence before being ordered to advance.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Israeli plan was to surprise the Egyptian forces in both timing (the attack exactly coinciding with the IAF strike on Egyptian airfields), and in location (attacking via northern and central Sinai routes, as opposed to the Egyptian expectations of a repeat of the 1956 war, when the IDF attacked via the central and southern routes) and method (using a combined-force flanking approach, rather than direct tank assaults).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Northern (El Arish) Israeli division

On 5 June, at 7:50 a.m., the northernmost Israeli division, consisting of three brigades and commanded by Major General [Israel Tal](/wiki/Israel_Tal "Israel Tal"), one of Israel's most prominent armor commanders, crossed the border at two points, opposite [Nahal Oz](/wiki/Nahal_Oz "Nahal Oz") and south of [Khan Yunis](/wiki/Khan_Yunis "Khan Yunis"). They advanced swiftly, holding their fire to maintain the element of surprise. Tal's forces assaulted the "Rafah Gap", an 7-mile (11 km) stretch containing the shortest of three main routes through the Sinai towards [El Qantara](/wiki/El_Qantara,_Egypt "El Qantara, Egypt") and the [Suez Canal](/wiki/Suez_Canal "Suez Canal"). The Egyptians had four divisions in the area, backed by minefields, pillboxes, underground bunkers, hidden gun emplacements and trenches. The terrain on either side of the route was impassable. The Israeli plan was to hit the Egyptians at selected key points with concentrated armor.[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOren2002178-110)

Tal's advance was led by the [7th Armored Brigade](/wiki/7th_Armored_Brigade_(Israel) "7th Armored Brigade (Israel)"), under Colonel [Shmuel Gonen](/wiki/Shmuel_Gonen "Shmuel Gonen"). The Israeli plan called for the 7th Brigade to outflank Khan Yunis from the north and the 60th Armored Brigade, under Colonel Menachem Aviram, to advance from the south. The two brigades would link up and surround Khan Yunis, while the paratroopers would take [Rafah](/wiki/Rafah "Rafah"). Gonen entrusted the breakthrough to a single battalion of his brigade.[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOren2002180-113)

Initially, the advance encountered light resistance, as Egyptian intelligence had concluded that it was a diversion for the main attack. As Gonen's lead battalion advanced, it suddenly came under intense fire and took heavy losses. A second battalion was brought up, but was also pinned down. Meanwhile, the 60th Brigade became bogged down in the sand, while the paratroopers had trouble navigating through the dunes. The Israelis continued to press their attack, and despite heavy losses, cleared the Egyptian positions and reached the [Khan Yunis](/wiki/Khan_Yunis "Khan Yunis") railway junction in a little over four hours.[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOren2002180-113)

Gonen's brigade then advanced 9 miles (14 km) to Rafah in twin columns. Rafah itself was circumvented, and the Israelis attacked [Sheikh Zuweid](/wiki/Sheikh_Zuweid "Sheikh Zuweid"), 8 miles (13 km) to the southwest, which was defended by two brigades. Though inferior in numbers and equipment, the Egyptians were deeply entrenched and camouflaged. The Israelis were pinned down by fierce Egyptian resistance and called in air and artillery support to enable their lead elements to advance. Many Egyptians abandoned their positions after their commander and several of his staff were killed.[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOren2002180-113)

The Israelis broke through with tank-led assaults, but Aviram's forces misjudged the Egyptians' flank and were pinned between strongholds before being extracted after several hours. By nightfall, the Israelis had finished mopping up resistance. The Israelis had taken significant losses, with Colonel Gonen later telling reporters that "we left many of our dead soldiers in Rafah and many burnt-out tanks." The Egyptians suffered some 2,000 casualties and lost 40 tanks.[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOren2002180-113)

#### Advance on Arish

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/6_Day_War-Amos.jpg/250px-6_Day_War-Amos.jpg)](/wiki/File:6_Day_War-Amos.jpg)

Israeli reconnaissance forces from the "Shaked" unit in Sinai during the war

On 5 June, with the road open, Israeli forces continued advancing towards [Arish](/wiki/Arish "Arish"). Already by late afternoon, elements of the 79th Armored Battalion had charged through the 7-mile-long (11 km) Jiradi defile, a narrow pass defended by well-emplaced troops of the Egyptian 112th Infantry Brigade. In fierce fighting, which saw the pass change hands several times, the Israelis charged through the position. The Egyptians suffered heavy casualties and tank losses, while Israeli losses stood at 66 dead, 93 wounded and 28 tanks. Emerging at the western end, Israeli forces advanced to the outskirts of Arish.[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOren2002181-114) As it reached the outskirts of Arish, Tal's division also consolidated its hold on Rafah and Khan Yunis.

The following day, 6 June, the Israeli forces on the outskirts of Arish were reinforced by the 7th Brigade, which fought its way through the Jiradi pass. After receiving supplies via an airdrop, the Israelis entered the city and captured the airport at 7:50 a.m. The Israelis entered the city at 8:00 a.m. Company commander [Yossi Peled](/wiki/Yossi_Peled "Yossi Peled") said: "Al-Arish was totally quiet, desolate. Suddenly, the city turned into a madhouse. Shots came at us from every alley, every corner, every window and house." An IDF record stated that "clearing the city was hard fighting. The Egyptians fired from the rooftops, from balconies and windows. They dropped grenades into our half-tracks and blocked the streets with trucks. Our men threw the grenades back and crushed the trucks with their tanks."[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOren2002202-115)[[115]](#cite_note-116) Gonen sent additional units to Arish, and the city was eventually taken.

Brigadier-General [Avraham Yoffe](/wiki/Avraham_Yoffe "Avraham Yoffe")'s assignment was to penetrate Sinai south of Tal's forces and north of Sharon's. Yoffe's attack allowed Tal to complete the capture of the Jiradi defile, Khan Yunis. All of them were taken after fierce fighting. Gonen subsequently dispatched a force of tanks, infantry and engineers under Colonel Yisrael Granit to continue down the Mediterranean coast towards the [Suez Canal](/wiki/Suez_Canal "Suez Canal"), while a second force led by Gonen himself turned south and captured Bir Lahfan and Jabal Libni.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

#### Mid-front (Abu-Ageila) Israeli division

See also: [Battle of Abu-Ageila (1967)](/wiki/Battle_of_Abu-Ageila_(1967) "Battle of Abu-Ageila (1967)")

Further south, on 6 June, the Israeli 38th Armored Division under Major-General [Ariel Sharon](/wiki/Ariel_Sharon "Ariel Sharon") assaulted [Um-Katef](/wiki/Um-Katef "Um-Katef"), a heavily fortified area defended by the Egyptian [2nd Infantry Division](/wiki/2nd_Infantry_Division_(Egypt) "2nd Infantry Division (Egypt)") under Major-General Sa'adi Naguib (though Naguib was actually absent[[116]](#cite_note-KandilSSS83-117)) of Soviet World War II armor, which included 90 [T-34-85](/wiki/T-34_tank "T-34 tank") tanks, 22 [SU-100](/wiki/SU-100 "SU-100") tank destroyers, and about 16,000 men. The Israelis had about 14,000 men and 150 post-World War II tanks including the [AMX-13](/wiki/AMX-13 "AMX-13"), [Centurions](/wiki/Centurion_tank "Centurion tank"), and [M50 Super Shermans](/wiki/M50_Super_Sherman "M50 Super Sherman") (modified [M-4 Sherman](/wiki/M-4_Sherman "M-4 Sherman") tanks).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Two armored brigades in the meantime, under Avraham Yoffe, slipped across the border through sandy wastes that Egypt had left undefended because they were considered impassable. Simultaneously, Sharon's tanks from the west were to engage Egyptian forces on Um-Katef ridge and block any reinforcements. Israeli infantry would clear the three trenches, while heliborne paratroopers would land behind Egyptian lines and silence their artillery. An armored thrust would be made at al-Qusmaya to unnerve and isolate its garrison.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

As Sharon's division advanced into the Sinai, Egyptian forces staged successful delaying actions at Tarat Umm, Umm Tarfa, and Hill 181. An Israeli jet was downed by antiaircraft fire, and Sharon's forces came under heavy shelling as they advanced from the north and west. The Israeli advance, which had to cope with extensive minefields, took a large number of casualties. A column of Israeli tanks managed to penetrate the northern flank of [Abu Ageila](/wiki/Battle_of_Abu-Ageila_(1967) "Battle of Abu-Ageila (1967)"), and by dusk, all units were in position. The Israelis then brought up ninety 105-mm and 155-mm artillery cannons for a preparatory barrage, while civilian buses brought reserve infantrymen under Colonel [Yekutiel Adam](/wiki/Yekutiel_Adam "Yekutiel Adam") and helicopters arrived to ferry the paratroopers. These movements were unobserved by the Egyptians, who were preoccupied with Israeli probes against their perimeter.[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOren2002182-118)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/AMX-13-latrun-2.jpg/250px-AMX-13-latrun-2.jpg)](/wiki/File:AMX-13-latrun-2.jpg)

Israeli armor of the Six-Day War: pictured here the [AMX 13](/wiki/AMX_13 "AMX 13")

As night fell, the Israeli assault troops lit flashlights, each battalion a different colour, to prevent [friendly fire](/wiki/Friendly_fire "Friendly fire") incidents. At 10:00 p.m., Israeli artillery began a barrage on Um-Katef, firing some 6,000 shells in less than 20 minutes, the most concentrated artillery barrage in Israel's history.[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDunstan2012125-119)[[119]](#cite_note-120) Israeli tanks assaulted the northernmost Egyptian defenses and were largely successful, though an entire armored brigade was stalled by mines, and had only one mine-clearance tank. Israeli infantrymen assaulted the triple line of trenches in the east. To the west, paratroopers commanded by Colonel [Danny Matt](/wiki/Danny_Matt "Danny Matt") landed behind Egyptian lines, though half the helicopters got lost and never found the battlefield, while others were unable to land due to mortar fire.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOren2002201-121)[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHammel1992239-122)

Those that successfully landed on target destroyed Egyptian artillery and ammunition dumps and separated gun crews from their batteries, sowing enough confusion to significantly reduce Egyptian artillery fire. Egyptian reinforcements from Jabal Libni advanced towards Um-Katef to counterattack but failed to reach their objective, being subjected to heavy air attacks and encountering Israeli lodgements on the roads. Egyptian commanders then called in artillery attacks on their own positions. The Israelis accomplished and sometimes exceeded their overall plan, and had largely succeeded by the following day. The Egyptians suffered about 2,000 casualties, while the Israelis lost 42 dead and 140 wounded.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOren2002201-121)[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHammel1992239-122)[[122]](#cite_note-123)

Yoffe's attack allowed Sharon to complete the capture of the Um-Katef, after fierce fighting. The main thrust at Um-Katef was stalled due to mines and craters. After IDF engineers had cleared a path by 4:00 pm, Israeli and Egyptian tanks engaged in fierce combat, often at ranges as close as ten yards. The battle ended in an Israeli victory, with 40 Egyptian and 19 Israeli tanks destroyed. Meanwhile, Israeli infantry finished clearing out the Egyptian trenches, with Israeli casualties standing at 14 dead and 41 wounded and Egyptian casualties at 300 dead and 100 taken prisoner.[[123]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOren2002212-124)

#### Other Israeli forces

Further south, on 5 June, the [8th Armored Brigade](/wiki/8th_Armored_Brigade_(Israel) "8th Armored Brigade (Israel)") under Colonel [Albert Mandler](/wiki/Albert_Mandler "Albert Mandler"), initially positioned as a ruse to draw off Egyptian forces from the real invasion routes, attacked the fortified bunkers at Kuntilla, a strategically valuable position whose capture would enable Mandler to block reinforcements from reaching Um-Katef and to join Sharon's upcoming attack on [Nakhl](/wiki/Nekhel "Nekhel"). The defending Egyptian battalion outnumbered and outgunned, fiercely resisted the attack, hitting several Israeli tanks. Most of the defenders were killed, and only three Egyptian tanks, one of them damaged, survived. By nightfall, Mandler's forces had taken Kuntilla.[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOren2002202-115)

With the exceptions of Rafah and Khan Yunis, Israeli forces had initially avoided entering the [Gaza Strip](/wiki/Gaza_Strip "Gaza Strip"). Israeli Defense Minister [Moshe Dayan](/wiki/Moshe_Dayan "Moshe Dayan") had expressly forbidden entry into the area. After Palestinian positions in Gaza opened fire on the Negev settlements of [Nirim](/wiki/Nirim "Nirim") and [Kissufim](/wiki/Kissufim "Kissufim"), IDF Chief of Staff [Yitzhak Rabin](/wiki/Yitzhak_Rabin "Yitzhak Rabin") overrode Dayan's instructions and ordered the 11th Mechanized Brigade under Colonel Yehuda Reshef to enter the Strip. The force was immediately met with heavy artillery fire and fierce resistance from Palestinian forces and remnants of the Egyptian forces from Rafah.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

By sunset, the Israelis had taken the strategically vital Ali Muntar ridge, overlooking [Gaza City](/wiki/Gaza_City "Gaza City"), but were beaten back from the city itself. Some 70 Israelis were killed, along with Israeli journalist Ben Oyserman and American journalist [Paul Schutzer](/wiki/Paul_Schutzer "Paul Schutzer"). Twelve members of [UNEF](/wiki/United_Nations_Emergency_Force "United Nations Emergency Force") were also killed. On the war's second day, 6 June, the Israelis were bolstered by the [35th Paratroopers Brigade](/wiki/Paratroopers_Brigade "Paratroopers Brigade") under Colonel [Rafael Eitan](/wiki/Rafael_Eitan "Rafael Eitan") and took Gaza City along with the entire Strip. The fighting was fierce and accounted for nearly half of all Israeli casualties on the southern front. Gaza rapidly fell to the Israelis.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Meanwhile, on 6 June, two Israeli reserve brigades under Yoffe, each equipped with 100 tanks, penetrated the Sinai south of Tal's division and north of Sharon's, capturing the road junctions of [Abu Ageila](/wiki/Abu_Ageila "Abu Ageila"), Bir Lahfan, and Arish, taking all of them before midnight. Two Egyptian armored brigades counterattacked, and a fierce battle took place until the following morning. The Egyptians were beaten back by fierce resistance coupled with airstrikes, sustaining heavy tank losses. They fled west towards Jabal Libni.[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOren2002211-125)

#### The Egyptian Army

During the ground fighting, remnants of the [Egyptian Air Force](/wiki/Egyptian_Air_Force "Egyptian Air Force") attacked Israeli ground forces but took losses from the Israeli Air Force and from Israeli anti-aircraft units. Throughout the last four days, Egyptian aircraft flew 150 sorties against Israeli units in the Sinai.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Many of the Egyptian units remained intact and could have tried to prevent the Israelis from reaching the [Suez Canal](/wiki/Suez_Canal "Suez Canal"), or engaged in combat in the attempt to reach the canal, but when the Egyptian Field Marshal [Abdel Hakim Amer](/wiki/Abdel_Hakim_Amer "Abdel Hakim Amer") heard about the [fall of Abu-Ageila](/wiki/Battle_of_Abu-Ageila_(1967) "Battle of Abu-Ageila (1967)"), he panicked and ordered all units in the Sinai to retreat. This order effectively meant the defeat of Egypt.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Meanwhile, President Nasser, having learned of the results of the Israeli air strikes, decided together with Field Marshal [Amer](/wiki/Abdel_Hakim_Amer "Abdel Hakim Amer") to order a general retreat from the Sinai within 24 hours. No detailed instructions were given concerning the manner and sequence of withdrawal.[[125]](#cite_note-ahram1-126)

#### Next fighting days
